name: Dog Photography for Owners: A Practical UK Guide 2026

step.name: Decide the scenario before you pick up the camera
step.text: Pick one of the five scenarios — garden portrait, walkies action, indoor light, beach/water, with-the-family. One scenario per session, different scenarios need different lenses and approaches.

step.name: Set up the camera while the dog is doing something else
step.text: All settings configured before the dog is involved. Test frame on a stationary object at correct distance. Don't make the dog wait while you fiddle.

step.name: Get the camera below dog eye-level
step.text: Kneel, sit, or lie flat depending on dog size. Single biggest improvement an owner can make. Use a kneeling mat or bin liner.

step.name: Three short rounds of three frames
step.text: For static portraits: dog into position, three frames in 30 seconds, release with treat. Do something else for 5 minutes. Repeat. Three short rounds beats one long marathon.

step.name: For action: pre-focus, then trigger
step.text: Pre-focus on a static reference at the same distance as the predicted action. AF-C with animal-eye detect, burst drive. Trigger when dog enters frame. Keeper is usually frame 3-5 of the burst, not frame 1.

step.name: Watch the body language and stop early
step.text: Whale eye, lip lick, head turn, lowered ears, raised paw, body shake — stop at first signal. End session while dog still enjoying it, not when they've quit.

step.name: Cull same day, edit lightly later
step.text: 1-3 keepers from a 9-frame static session, 2-4 from 30+ frame burst. Cull same day. Edit next evening: lift shadows, gentle clarity, light dodge on near eye. Don't push saturation.
